topic: A musician hears their song being performed by someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence of my studio was usually a comforting blanket, but tonight it felt brittle. I was tweaking the final mix of "Echo Bloom," a track that had been pouring my soul onto the digital canvas for months. Then, through the thin walls, I heard it—a familiar melody, undeniably mine, being played live. My hand froze mid-air above the fader. It was a cello, rich and resonant, weaving the intricate harmonies of my composition with breathtaking accuracy. A cold knot tightened in my stomach. Who could possibly know this piece so intimately? I slowly rose, my movements stiff, and crept toward the window, peering through the gap in the blinds. The street below was empty, yet the music persisted, a phantom presence echoing from an unseen venue.

[story 2]

I was nursing lukewarm coffee, staring blankly at the lyric sheet, when the sound drifted in. It started subtly, a recognizable chord progression, and then swelled into the full arrangement of my latest ballad. My breath hitched. I dropped my pen; it clattered loudly on the hardwood floor. My pulse quickened, a frantic drumbeat against my ribs. I forced myself to stand, my legs feeling strangely unsteady. I moved to the door, my knuckles white as I gripped the frame. I needed to see it, needed confirmation. Pushing the door open, I found myself in a dimly lit bar, and there, bathed in the amber glow of a spotlight, was a figure playing my song with an effortless, almost predatory grace. My jaw tightened, and I couldn't manage a coherent word.

[story 3]

The headphones were on, the track looping silently, but the sound bled through the insulation, clear and vibrant. It was my song, the one I’d poured sleepless nights into, being rendered by someone else’s fingers. A wave of profound unease washed over me, chilling me to the bone despite the warmth of the room. I pulled the headphones off, my fingers trembling as I reached for the speaker. The music stopped instantly, leaving a ringing void. I walked to the window, my gaze fixed on the darkened alleyway across the street. Every shadow seemed to shift, every distant sound amplified in my heightened state of awareness. I watched, motionless, as a silhouette moved across the pavement, and a deep, unsettling certainty settled in my chest—that this performance was not accidental.
